title: What Is a Phase II Archaeological Evaluation?
content:
When a Phase II archaeological evaluation is triggered during a construction or infrastructure project, many project managers and developers are uncertain about what the process entails, how long it takes, and what the findings mean for their timeline. Understanding Phase II is essential for federal agencies, state transportation departments, and private developers navigating Section 106 compliance under the National Historic Preservation Act (NHPA).

Quick Answer: A Phase II archaeological evaluation is an intensive subsurface investigation of a site identified during Phase I survey. Archaeologists use systematic test units, artifact analysis, and historical research to determine whether the site meets criteria for listing on the National Register of Historic Places, which directly affects project planning decisions.

Phase II is the second of three standard phases in cultural resource management (CRM) archaeology. It is triggered when a Phase I survey identifies a site that may be significant—one whose boundaries, integrity, and research potential are not yet fully understood. The evaluation provides the data necessary for a State Historic Preservation Office (SHPO) or federal agency to make an eligibility determination before the project moves forward.

Why Is Phase II Evaluation Required?

Phase II is required because federal law mandates that agencies identify and evaluate historic properties before approving projects that use federal permits, licenses, or funding. The evaluation ensures that significant archaeological sites are not inadvertently destroyed without prior review and consultation.

Under Section 106 of the National Historic Preservation Act, federal agencies must take into account the effect of their undertakings on historic properties. A Phase II archaeological evaluation is the mechanism used to determine whether a site qualifies as a historic property under the National Register's criteria—significance related to history, architecture, archaeology, engineering, or culture.

Without a completed Phase II, agencies cannot legally proceed with the project in areas where potentially significant sites have been identified. Skipping or shortcutting this step can result in project delays, legal challenges, and costly mitigation requirements discovered late in construction.

What Methods Do Archaeologists Use in Phase II?

Phase II investigations rely on systematic, scientifically controlled excavation methods designed to reveal the depth, extent, and integrity of a site's cultural deposits. Archaeologists open a series of hand-excavated test units, typically 1×1 meter or 1×2 meter squares, placed at intervals across the site based on findings from Phase I.

All soil from test units is screened through wire mesh—commonly 1/4-inch hardware cloth—to recover artifacts, ecofacts such as botanical remains or faunal bone, and features like hearths, post holes, or pit features. Stratigraphic profiles are drawn and photographed for each unit to document the vertical sequence of cultural and natural deposits.

Specialized analyses may include radiocarbon dating of organic materials, ceramic typology, lithic analysis, faunal identification, and geomorphological assessment. GIS and spatial analysis are often used to map artifact distributions and identify patterns within the site. All recovered materials are cataloged, analyzed, and curated according to state and federal standards.

What Does a Phase II Report Conclude?

The final deliverable of a Phase II archaeological evaluation is a technical report submitted to the relevant SHPO and lead federal agency. The report presents all field data, laboratory results, and a formal recommendation regarding the site's eligibility for the National Register of Historic Places.

The eligibility recommendation is based on the National Register Criteria for Evaluation (Criteria A–D). Most archaeological sites are evaluated under Criterion D—sites that have yielded or may yield information important to history or prehistory. The report must demonstrate that the site retains sufficient integrity to convey its significance and that it contains enough data potential to warrant nomination.

Possible conclusions include: eligible (the site is significant and must be avoided or mitigated), not eligible (the project may proceed in that area without further archaeological restrictions), or indeterminate (additional work is needed). The SHPO and federal agency make the final eligibility determination based on the submitted report.

How Long Does a Phase II Evaluation Take?

The duration of a Phase II archaeological evaluation depends on site size, complexity, soil conditions, and the volume of materials recovered. Field work for a moderately sized site typically takes one to three weeks, while laboratory analysis and report preparation can require an additional four to twelve weeks.

Projects with deep or stratified deposits, large artifact assemblages, or complex feature sets require more extensive analysis and take longer. Regulatory review by the SHPO and lead federal agency adds additional time, often four to eight weeks after report submission, before a formal eligibility determination is issued.

Early coordination with the lead agency, SHPO, and tribes (where applicable) is the most effective way to streamline Phase II timelines. Establishing agreement on scope, methods, and reporting requirements before fieldwork begins prevents costly revisions and back-and-forth during the review process. Historic preservation specialists familiar with state-specific SHPO expectations are invaluable in this planning stage.

What Happens After Phase II Is Complete?

Once the SHPO and lead federal agency concur on eligibility, the project moves into one of two paths. If the site is determined not eligible, no further archaeological work is required for that location and the project may proceed. If the site is determined eligible, Section 106 consultation continues to assess the project's effect on the historic property.

An eligible site that cannot be avoided will typically require a Phase III data recovery investigation—a large-scale excavation designed to systematically recover and document the site's scientific information before it is impacted. Phase III is the most intensive and expensive phase of archaeological work and is required only when avoidance is not feasible and the site is determined eligible.

Project design changes are often explored first. Shifting footprints, realigning roads, or redesigning utility corridors to avoid significant sites is frequently more cost-effective than conducting Phase III. TVAR's team works closely with project engineers and planners to identify avoidance options that preserve both project viability and cultural resources.

Frequently Asked Questions

What triggers a Phase II archaeological evaluation?

A Phase II evaluation is triggered when a Phase I survey identifies an archaeological site that appears potentially significant—meaning it may meet National Register eligibility criteria. The lead federal agency and SHPO review Phase I findings and determine whether Phase II investigation is warranted before project activities can proceed in that area.

Who pays for Phase II archaeological work?

Typically the project proponent—the developer, transportation agency, or permit applicant—funds Phase II investigations. Federal regulations do not require the government to pay for this work; responsibility falls on the entity seeking the permit, license, or funding that triggers Section 106 review. Costs vary by site size and complexity.

Can a project proceed if a Phase II site is found eligible?

Yes, but additional steps are required. If a site is determined eligible and the project will adversely affect it, the agency must consult with the SHPO and other parties to resolve the adverse effect, usually through avoidance, minimization, or Phase III data recovery before project activities can impact the site.

What is the difference between Phase I and Phase II archaeology?

Phase I is a reconnaissance survey that identifies archaeological sites within a project area. Phase II is a more intensive evaluation of specific sites identified during Phase I to determine their National Register eligibility. Phase I locates sites; Phase II determines whether those sites are significant enough to require protection or mitigation.

How is Phase II different from Phase III data recovery?

Phase II uses limited test excavations to evaluate whether a site is eligible for the National Register. Phase III is a comprehensive data recovery excavation conducted after a site is confirmed eligible and unavoidable, designed to document as much scientific information as possible before the site is impacted by construction or development.

Ready to Start Your Phase II Evaluation?

A well-executed Phase II archaeological evaluation protects your project from costly surprises by delivering clear, defensible eligibility determinations accepted by SHPOs and federal agencies. Delays caused by inadequate Phase II work are far more expensive than getting it right the first time.

Tennessee Valley Archaeological Research brings RPA-certified archaeologists, 14+ years of CRM experience, and deep familiarity with Southeast US regulatory requirements to every Phase II project. Our team coordinates with state and federal agencies from project scoping through final report approval to keep your schedule on track.

excerpt: A Phase II archaeological evaluation determines if a site is eligible for the National Register of Historic Places through targeted excavation, artifact analysis, and research. Learn what to expect.
